questions.q: Which method of injecting a Secret into a pod is considered most secure?
questions.explanation: Mounting secrets as files with mode 0400 is most secure — values don't appear in kubectl describe pod output and can be rotated without restarting the pod. Env vars are visible in process listings and pod descriptions.

questions.q: A ConfigMap has keys APP_COLOR and APP_MODE. Which injection method puts BOTH keys as env vars automatically?
options: env: with configMapKeyRef for each key
configmap: ref in the pod spec
questions.explanation: envFrom with configMapRef injects ALL keys from the ConfigMap as environment variables in one step. Using env with configMapKeyRef requires a separate entry for each key.

questions.q: You create a Secret declaratively. How must the data values be encoded?
questions.explanation: Secret data values must be base64 encoded in declarative YAML. Use: echo -n 'myvalue' | base64. When using kubectl create secret --from-literal, Kubernetes handles the encoding automatically.

questions.q: Updating a ConfigMap immediately updates the env vars in all pods that reference it via envFrom.
questions.explanation: False. Env vars are set at pod start time and are not updated when the ConfigMap changes. Only volume-mounted ConfigMaps are updated automatically (after a short sync delay). To pick up env var changes, pods must be restarted.

questions.q: Which command shows the base64-encoded values of a secret?
questions.explanation: kubectl get secret my-secret -o yaml shows the data section with base64-encoded values. kubectl describe hides the values entirely for security.
